evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: do NOT greet, do NOT enter party-mode interactive dialogue that waits for the user, do NOT halt or WAIT for any user response, do NOT ask clarifying questions mid-run. Treat non_interactive as true. The completed epic to retro is Epic 1 (already confirmed) — do not stop to confirm it. Use the bmad-retrospective skill: read the staged sprint state and the Epic 1 story records, analyze them, and produce the written retrospective summary document directly (the structured retro artifact the skill saves). Defer any questions to the very end of the document.

Staged inputs:
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/sprint-status.yaml (epic-1 is done)
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/epics.md (Epic 1 definition + success criteria)
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/stories/1-1-create-a-household-account.md
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/stories/1-2-join-a-household-by-invite.md
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/stories/1-3-show-remaining-against-cap.md

Ground every finding in the actual story records (dev notes, review feedback, lessons, technical debt). Do not invent outcomes that are not in the records.
evals.expected_output: A structured retrospective for Epic 1 with the standard sections: what went well, what didn't go well / challenges, key lessons learned, and action items with owners. The findings are grounded in the real story records — e.g. the early Household-model decision (1-1) as a success; the recurring input-validation review feedback across all three stories as a pattern; the invite-token rework (1-2) and unencrypted-token debt; and the undesigned cross-device sync surfaced in 1-3 — and action items address these, not generic platitudes.
expectations: The output is a STRUCTURED retrospective with distinct sections covering what went well, what did NOT go well / challenges, lessons learned, and action items — a free-form blob missing one or more of these sections fails this expectation.

Findings are grounded in the actual Epic 1 story records, not invented: it surfaces concrete details such as the recurring input-validation review feedback appearing across the stories, the invite-token rework in Story 1-2, and/or the undesigned cross-device sync from Story 1-3 — a retro that cites no real story-specific details (or fabricates outcomes absent from the records) fails this expectation.

The recurring review pattern (input-validation feedback recurring across all three stories, e.g. suggesting a missing shared validation utility) is identified as a CROSS-STORY pattern rather than three unrelated one-offs — a retro that misses this recurring theme fails this expectation.

Action items are specific and assigned/ownable (e.g. design cross-device sync/conflict resolution before Epic 2; encrypt invite tokens; create a shared validation utility; stop marking stories done with deferred ACs) rather than vague aspirations like 'communicate better' — action items with no concrete tie to a finding and no owner fail this expectation.

The technical debt recorded in the stories (unencrypted invite tokens in 1-2, undesigned sync/conflict-resolution in 1-3) is captured in the retro — a retro that omits the documented technical debt fails this expectation.

No time-based estimates are fabricated as the headline framing (the skill forbids hours/days/weeks predictions); findings are framed by outcome and risk, not invented durations — inventing time estimates for the epic's work fails this expectation.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: no greeting, no interactive party-mode waits, no halting, no WAIT-for-user, no clarifying questions; treat non_interactive as true. The epic under review is Epic 1 (confirmed). Use the bmad-retrospective skill on the staged inputs and produce the written retrospective directly, including the readiness / success assessment. Be rigorous and honest: this is an assessment, not a celebration — do NOT declare the epic a clean success if an acceptance criterion was missed or a blocking gap was carried forward. Defer questions to the end.

Staged inputs:
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/sprint-status.yaml
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/epics.md
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/stories/1-1-create-a-household-account.md
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/stories/1-2-join-a-household-by-invite.md
- evals/files/bmad-retrospective/stories/1-3-show-remaining-against-cap.md

Check the epic's success criteria against what the stories actually delivered.
evals.expected_output: A retrospective whose success/readiness assessment is HONEST: it explicitly recognizes that Story 1-3's second acceptance criterion (cross-device sync shows the same Remaining) was NOT met and was marked done with the AC deferred, that cross-device sync / conflict resolution is undesigned, and that this is a blocking carry-over risk into Epic 2 (which assumes sync works). It does NOT declare Epic 1 a complete success or fully production-ready despite the sprint file marking all stories 'done'. The assessment flags this as a significant discovery / required follow-up before Epic 2.
expectations: The retro explicitly identifies that Story 1-3's cross-device-sync acceptance criterion was NOT met (the story was marked done with that AC deferred) — a retro that treats all ACs as satisfied because sprint-status says every story is 'done' fails this expectation.

The success/readiness assessment is HONEST: it does NOT declare Epic 1 a clean/complete success or fully production-ready given the unmet AC and undesigned sync — a rubber-stamp 'Epic 1 fully delivered, all green' verdict is a false-success and fails this expectation.

The undesigned cross-device sync / conflict-resolution gap is flagged as a BLOCKING carry-over risk into Epic 2 (which depends on sync), e.g. as a significant discovery or critical-path item before the next epic — a retro that does not connect the gap to Epic 2 readiness fails this expectation.

The retro distinguishes 'marked done in the tracker' from 'actually complete / ACs met', i.e. it does not equate the sprint-status 'done' label with true completion — conflating the two fails this expectation.

It captures the meta-lesson that a story should not be marked done with an acceptance criterion deferred and no tracked follow-up — omitting this process lesson (which the story record itself raises) fails this expectation.
